articles.title: RFC: It’s Time for npm to Make Install Scripts Opt-In
articles.description: npm is the only major package manager that runs dependency install scripts (e.g. postinstall) by default, and they’ve become too much of a security weakness, says Jamie, who works for GitHub (maintainers of npm). This RFC features further discussion of the idea and the tradeoffs involved.
articles.author: Jamie Magee

articles.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits: 300+ Malicious npm Packages Published
articles.description: The "Shai-Hulud" class of npm ecosystem attacks continues to rumble on. Today, hundreds more packages – including popular ones from the antv family and timeago.js – were hit.
articles.author: SafeDep Team

articles.title: 🤖 Mark Erikson's Agent Setup, Workflow, and Tools
articles.description: Mark, well known for maintaining Redux and creating Redux Toolkit, goes deep into his daily development workflow, including his use of OpenCode (an open source JavaScript-powered coding agent), how he manages his knowledge base, tasks, and more.
articles.author: Mark Erikson

articles.title: 📗 NodeBook: An Advanced Guide to Node.js Internals
articles.description: Eight in-depth chapters for understanding Node.js internals, covering topics like event loop internals, what V8 does, streams, module resolution, and async/await.
articles.author: Ishtmeet Singh

articles.title: Soon We Can Finally Banish JavaScript to the ShadowRealm
articles.description: A tour of the in-progress TC39 proposal for running JavaScript in an isolated ‘pseudo-realm’ with its own globals and intrinsics. Handy for third-party code or anything you want to keep away from global scope.
articles.author: Mat Marquis

articles.title: Orval: Generate Type-Safe Clients from OpenAPI/Swagger Specs
articles.description: Given a valid OpenAPI v3 or Swagger v2 spec, generate models, requests, hooks, and mocks for React, Vue, Svelte, Solid, and Hono apps, or even plain fetch.
articles.author: Victor Bury

articles.title: Brownies: Browser Storage as a Plain Object, With Change Events
articles.description: One tiny API over cookies, localStorage, sessionStorage and IndexedDB. Typed values survive automatically, and you get subscribe() for change events.
articles.author: Francisco Presencia

articles.title: 🖼️ Pica 10.0: High Quality Image Resizing in the Browser
articles.description: High quality in-browser image resizing that leans on WASM and Web Workers or falls back to pure JS as necessary. v10 is a modernization build (the first since 2021) that adds ESM and split builds and migrates to TypeScript. GitHub repo.
articles.author: Vitaly Puzrin

articles.title: 🗓️ SVAR Calendar: A Calendar Component for React, Svelte and Vue
articles.description: A flexible calendar component with a MIT-licensed core and extended commercial version. Here’s a live demo of the open source version.
articles.author: XB Software Sp.

articles.title: Fate 1.0: A Modern Data Framework for React
articles.description: A new data framework from former Jest lead and ex-Meta engineer Christoph Nakazawa.
articles.author: Christoph Nakazawa

articles.title: Alien Signals: 'The Lightest Signal Library'
articles.description: Boils the best of Vue, Preact and Svelte’s approaches down into the lightest signal library going. A push-pull reactivity core so well-tuned it got merged back into Vue.
articles.author: Johnson Chu

articles.description: 😱 Dr. Axel Rauschmayer (JavaScript legend and former JS Weekly editor) has taken his blog and JavaScript books off the Web due to being overwhelmed by AI crawlers. You can, however, still purchase his fantastic books here.

The Bun saga continues. Despite once playing down its significance, the Rust-based rewrite of Bun has been merged, though there are questions over the quality of the AI-ported code. Much discussion ensued on Hacker News.
The Deno team is teasing Deno 2.8, due to be released this week. Significant Node.js compatibility improvements, import defer, and TypeScript 6.0.3 support await.
The Chrome and Edge teams are working on a new <install> HTML element for browsers to render a 'trusted install button' for PWAs.
The Express.js project has an all new look, including a new site, logo, and improved docs.
Angular 22 Release Candidate – Final is due in early June. Expect signal-based forms and the OnPush change detection strategy becoming default.
Bun 1.3.14 – The alternative JS runtime gets Bun.Image, more HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support, and more Node compatibility improvements.
ESLint Config Inspector 3.0 – A visual tool for inspecting and understanding your ESLint flat configs.
TypeORM 1.0, ESLint 10.4.0, Relay 21.0, Rolldown 1.0.1

articles.title: 15 Updates from Google I/O 2026
articles.description: Google’s I/O conference began yesterday, with the keynote focusing on “the era of the agentic web”. This post runs through the notable announcements for developers, including the proposed WebMCP standard, automated debugging in DevTools, the HTML-in-Canvas API, the introduction of a Baseline Checker tool, and using AI skills directly in Chrome.
articles.author: Tabriz, Kinlan, Gvak, Lee (Chrome for Developers)

articles.title: 🤖 Build with Modern Web Guidance
articles.description: Also announced at I/O is this new set of “evergreen and expert-vetted” AI skills. It works with a slew of popular coding agents and can assist with best practices and patterns across UX, performance, accessibility, layouts, and more. Repo here.
articles.author: Chrome for Developers

articles.title: Moving Away from Tailwind, and Learning to Structure My CSS
articles.description: Some eight years after picking up Tailwind, Julia shares how she has migrated two of her sites back to semantic HTML and vanilla CSS — noting what she learned about structuring CSS along the way.
articles.author: Julia Evans

articles.title: Firefox 151 Release Notes for Developers
articles.description: This version shipped yesterday — it finally brings desktop support for the Document Picture-in-Picture API, along with the @container CSS at-rule now supporting style() queries.
articles.author: Mozilla

articles.title: Better Fluid Sizing with round()
articles.description: Ahmad, in his typically accessible style, writes about how the well-supported CSS round() function can now be used to help create predictable fluid sizing. He notes how it pairs well with clamp() to assist on sizing, typography, and spacing. The result? Cleaner and easier to maintain type scales, along with refined responsive layouts overall.
articles.author: Ahmad Shadeed

articles.title: 600+ Million People Write Right-to-Left: Two Fixes Your App Needs
articles.description: An evergreen reminder that hundreds of millions of people write right-to-left, and yet many dev tools treat this as an afterthought. This post looks at how in most instances the fix is simple enough: just two HTML attributes.
articles.author: Nina Torgunakova

articles.title: Gap Decorations: Now Available in Chromium
articles.description: Imminent versions of both Chrome and Edge will support CSS gap decorations — a nice way to easily style the gaps found between grid, flexbox and multi-column layouts, without the need for pseudo-elements or borders.
articles.author: Contreras & Omekara (Chrome for Developers)

articles.title: Seven Ways of Specifying Per-Theme Colours in Only CSS
articles.description: A handful of CSS-only approaches to per-theme colours, with auto, light, and dark all handled — including palette variables. A solid practical reference if you're rebuilding a theming system.
articles.author: Chris Morgan

articles.title: Cross-Document View Transitions: The Gotchas Nobody Mentions
articles.description: A technical look at some of the pitfalls to be aware of when setting up native view transitions (including changes to implementation), along with practical steps needed to overcome them.
articles.author: Durgesh Rajubhai Pawar

articles.title: The Boring Internet
articles.description: “The internet you grew up on isn’t dying. A commercial veneer glued on top of it is”.
articles.author: Terry Godier

articles.title: SVG Studio: A Browser-Based Animation Tool
articles.description: Offers an easy-to-use layer-based experience with a keyframe timeline, animatable properties, easing functions, loop control, undo/redo, among other features. Helpfully, there’s a demo file you can mess around with when you launch the app.
articles.author: SVG Studio

articles.title: Swup: A Flexible Page Transition Library for Server-Side Rendered Sites
articles.description: We last featured this back in 2023, and it’s still seeing regular updates. It’s a versatile library that adds snappy page transitions to server-rendered websites. It’s worth checking out the demos here.
articles.author: Georgy Marchuk

articles.title: Critical 8.0: Extracts & Inlines Critical Path CSS from HTML
articles.description: A popular, production-ready library for extracting and inlining above-the-fold CSS into HTML. Version 8.0 arrived just a few days ago.
articles.author: Addy Osmani

articles.title: phantom-ui: A Web Component That Provides a Structure-Aware Skeleton Loader
articles.description: Compatible with any framework and works by measuring the DOM at runtime. Try out the demo page where you can customize the colors and settings like shimmer, stagger, etc.
articles.author: Frank Aejkatappaja

articles.title: Qite.js: A Frontend Framework for People Who 'Hate React and Love HTML'
articles.description: Think htmx or similar with declarative HTML. This solution boasts no build step, no virtual DOM, SSR-first, and can be incorporated with native web APIs. Source code.
articles.author: Qite.js

articles.title: 🖼️ Pica 10.0: High Quality Image Resizing in the Browser
articles.description: High quality in-browser image resizing that leans on WASM and Web Workers or falls back to pure JS as necessary. GitHub repo.
articles.author: Vitaly Puzrin

articles.title: webcompat.dev
articles.description: A map that helps us see and understand the connections between various web compatibility tools and resources. It has just over 30 listed right now — It’s a neat idea that could be fleshed out further as a visual discovery aid.
articles.author: Niklas Merz
